coredata.dc:title: Activated carbon modified by Mo-doped CQDs: An efficient method to reduce the thermal hazard in toluene adsorption
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Ren, Hongwei
Dong, Hongyu
Zhao, Tengda
Yan, Zhongqing
Jia, Benzhen
Han, Liang
Zhang, Jinfeng
coredata.dc:description: Activated carbon (AC) is one of the typical adsorbents for industrial treatment of VOCs, but AC itself has a high calorific value. When the heat generated by low-temperature oxidation cannot be released, it is easy to store heat and oxidize spontaneously, resulting in immeasurable harm. Therefore, it is important to study and improve the thermal stability of AC. In this study, Mo-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was synthesized to enhance the safety of AC usage. The structure of AC before and after modification was characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray diffractometer (XRD). On the modified Mo-doped CQDs/AC, there are more crystalline structures formed by Mo oxides on the surface, which makes the intermolecular force on the surface of AC increase. At the same time, Mo oxide has strong oxidation resistance, which can inhibit the thermal decomposition process of AC, and has stronger stability. The spontaneous combustion tendency and thermal hazard of AC before and after modification were greatly improved. The heat released in the toluene adsorption by the modified AC at different temperatures was significantly lower than the heat released by the unmodified AC. According to the Langmuir adsorption isotherm, the adsorption performance of Mo-doped CQDs (2.5 wt%) was 1.6 times higher than that of AC when the system was 25 °C. The results showed that Na2MoO4 CQDs modified AC could effectively improve the thermal stability and greatly reduce the spontaneous combustion tendency. A relative fire safety performance evaluation model was also established to comprehensively evaluate the fire risk of VOCs treatment by AC.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Activated carbon
Modification
Thermostability
Thermal safety

Experiment on calorimetric isotherm of toluene adsorption
Experiment on the spontaneous combustion point

Thermodynamic analysis of oxidative decomposition of activated carbon before and after modification
Fire safety evaluation of toluene adsorption on activated carbon before and after modification

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Experimental equipment for toluene adsorption by AC. (1. N2 cylinder; 2. Valve; 3. Flow meter; 4. Organic gas generator; 5. Gas mixing chamber; 6. Constant temperature water bath; 7. Activated carbon 8. Constant temperature device; 9. Gas chromatography; 10. Tail gas system).
(a) TEM image and (b) particle size distribution of carbon quantum dots.
(a) Survey XPS spectrum of carbon quantum dots, and the high resolution of (b) C 1s, (c) O 1s, and (d) Mo3d5 peaks.
TEM image of (a) carbon quantum dots, (b) the modified AC, and (c) and (d) the different magnification of the modified AC.
FTIR spectrum of AC and AC/CQDs.
XRD patterns of AC and AC/CQDs.
Thermogravimetric curves of AC and AC/CQDs adsorption of toluene with different content of CQDs (0.5 wt%, 1 wt%, 1.5 wt%, 2 wt%, and 2.5 wt%, respectively).
Adsorption heat flow curve of unmodified AC at different temperatures.
Adsorption heat flow curve of AC/CQDs at different temperatures.
Variation trend of spontaneous combustion temperature of AC with CQDs content.
(a) TG curves of the unmodified AC and(b) DTG curves of the unmodified AC.
Log (β) fits a straight line with Tp−1.
(a) TG curves of AC/CQDs and (b) DTG curves of AC/CQDs.
Log (β) fits a straight line with Tp−1.
Schematic diagram of the preparation route of activated carbon modified by Mo-doped CQDs.
Adsorption isotherm fitting results of AC and AC/CQDs with different content of CQDs for toluene.
Toluene adsorption performance evaluation of different absorbents.
Thermal stability parameters of unmodified AC.
Thermal stability parameters of AC/CQDs.
Thermodynamic parameters of AC before and after modification.

head.ce:title: Activated carbon modified by Mo-doped CQDs: An efficient method to reduce the thermal hazard in toluene adsorption

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Activated carbon (AC) is one of the typical adsorbents for industrial treatment of VOCs, but AC itself has a high calorific value. When the heat generated by low-temperature oxidation cannot be released, it is easy to store heat and oxidize spontaneously, resulting in immeasurable harm. Therefore, it is important to study and improve the thermal stability of AC. In this study, Mo-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was synthesized to enhance the safety of AC usage. The structure of AC before and after modification was characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray diffractometer (XRD). On the modified Mo-doped CQDs/AC, there are more crystalline structures formed by Mo oxides on the surface, which makes the intermolecular force on the surface of AC increase. At the same time, Mo oxide has strong oxidation resistance, which can inhibit the thermal decomposition process of AC, and has stronger stability. The spontaneous combustion tendency and thermal hazard of AC before and after modification were greatly improved. The heat released in the toluene adsorption by the modified AC at different temperatures was significantly lower than the heat released by the unmodified AC. According to the Langmuir adsorption isotherm, the adsorption performance of Mo-doped CQDs (2.5 wt%) was 1.6 times higher than that of AC when the system was 25 °C. The results showed that Na2MoO4 CQDs modified AC could effectively improve the thermal stability and greatly reduce the spontaneous combustion tendency. A relative fire safety performance evaluation model was also established to comprehensively evaluate the fire risk of VOCs treatment by AC.

ce:section.ce:para: Activated carbon (AC) is a kind of carbon material with strong adsorption property prepared by pyrolysis and activation process. It is widely used in biological science, sewage treatment, gas purification and other fields, especially in industrial treatment and recovery of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) due to its renewable and high economic benefits [1–3]. Unfortunately, AC has a high calorific value and keeps accumulating heat during the process of adsorption and slow oxidation. When the adsorption system accumulates a large number of flammable VOCs, spontaneous combustion accidents are very likely to occur. [4–5]. Once AC burns, it will not only release a lot of heat, break the equipment and building structure, but also make the adsorption system fail. Thus, organic waste gas would leak, resulting in immeasurable harm [6]. Therefore, the flammability of AC greatly hinders its practical application in gas adsorption.

The risk of AC in VOCs treatment has always been a research hotspot of scholars at home and abroad. Previous studies mainly focused on the spontaneous combustion factors and combustion characteristics of AC [7–11]. Xu [12] conducted thermal analysis of three different AC, and used the first-order reaction equation and Coats-Redfern model to study the thermal kinetics of oxidation. It was concluded that the tendency of spontaneous combustion with low activation energy was high, and vice versa. Lei [13] established a fire safety performance evaluation model by studying the oxidation and pyrolysis behavior of materials and applying activation energy, Tp, DTGp and high-speed reaction percentage as fire safety evaluation factors. Pan et al. [14] studied the heat transfer and thermal weight loss in the oxidation of AC, and provided a theoretical basis for the prevention of AC spontaneous combustion. Modification of AC is the most effective method to improve the thermal safety of activated carbon. At present, the modification methods mainly include acid-base modification, microwave modification, and chemical modification, and so on [15–20]. Deng et al. [21] used KOH as modifier to modify coconut shell AC by impregnation. It was found that benzene adsorption by AC mainly occurred in the micropores on the solid surface, and the adsorption effect decreased with the increase of temperature. For the microwave modification, Zou [22] found that the oxygen-containing functional groups on the AC surface were reduced, the basicity and hydrophobicity was enhanced, and the original pore structure was maintained. Zhang et al. [23] used poplar powder and zinc chloride as activators to prepare AC. The study found that, with the increase of activation temperature, the TG and DSC curves of AC moved to the high temperature region, the oxidative ability of AC became weaker, and the oxidative spontaneous combustion decreased. Bogdan et al. [24] believed that the introduction of MeOx would generally lead to higher conversion of carbonaceous catalysts, but would lead to reduced selectivity and stability at higher temperature. Up to now, the adsorption efficiency has been greatly improved. However, these studies mostly focus on the adsorption ability, the risk of adsorbing flammable gases by AC is unfortunately ignored. It is of great significance to study the combustion risk of AC on the basis of ensuring the adsorption efficiency [25–28].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new type of carbon nanomaterials, and have been widely used in many industries in recent years because of their high stability, easy surface modification, and low cost. CQDs have quasi-spherical morphology and nanoscale characteristic size. They show potential impregnation modification applications [29–36]. Liu et al. [37] modified polyurethane foam with CQDs, and the study showed that the polymer foam exhibited excellent thermal stability with the increase of CQDs content. Khose et al. [38] used graphene CQDs as an additive for the cloth, and experiments proved that the loading of graphene CQDs extended the initial ignition time of the cloth from 5 s to 300 s, and could prevent the secondary ignition of the cloth. Molybdenum Compounds (Mo) have been found to an excellent material to improve the thermal stability of catalyst [39,40]. Guo et al. [41] utilized the synergistic effect of molybdenum compounds and graphene nanoplatelets to enhance the thermal stability and exothermic suppression effect of ethylene–vinyl acetate. Zhou et al. [42] found that MoS2 can be well dispersed in polystyrene and poly methyl methacrylate, and the good compatibility between MoS2 and the polymer matrix makes the composites thermal stability was improved remarkably. Therefore, the doping introduction of CQDs (Mo-doped CQDs), especially, sodium molybdate compound dropped CQDs, would be potential green modifier of AC. It can optimize the surface chemical structure and pore structure, and provide enough reactive sites, so as to achieve the purpose of increasing the adsorption capacity and reducing the risk of reaction [43,44]. At the same time, the high thermal stability of Sodium molybdate compounds would greatly improve the safety of AC through the synergistic effect of sodium molybdate and CQDs.

In this study, Mo-doped CQD with high stability and abundant surface functional groups were prepared by combustion method using a kind of green-emerging deep eutectic solvent (DES) as the precursor [45–48]. It was used as the modifier of AC, and the thermal analysis was used to study the spontaneous combustion tendency of the VOCs adsorption process by AC, and the thermal stability was also explored.

AC (mesh size of 40–80, a particle size fraction of 63–100 μm) was purchased from Tianjin Yongda Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Choline chloride (C5H14CINO, 98.0%, Aladdin), glycerol (C3H8O3, 99.0%, Aladdin), and sodium molybdate (Na2MoO4·2H2O, 99.0%, Aladdin) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology CO., Ltd. All materials and reagents are analytically pure and used directly without further purification.

The size and distribution of CQDs in the solution were characterized by transmission electron microscope (JEM-2100, Hitachi, Japan) under 200 kV acceleration voltage. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were obtained using an ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with an Al K Alpha (hv = 1340 eV) in standard lens mode (Thermo Fischer, Waltham, USA). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (Bruker Tensor 27, Germany) was used to characterize the surface functional groups of CQDs and carbon quantum dots-modified activated carbon in the frequency range of 4000–400 cm−1. XRD patterns were obtained using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD-7000, Shimadzu, Japan) with a scanning range of 20–80°. The thermal stability of activated carbon before and after CQDs modification was analyzed by synchronous thermal analyzer (STA 449 F5, Netzsch, Germany). The spontaneous combustion point of activated carbon was measured by solid spontaneous combustion point tester (HWP22-10E, Hangzhou Young Science and Technology Co., Ltd., China) in standard mode. Miniature reaction calorimeter（THT-μRC, UK）was used to record the heat change in the adsorption process of toluene on activated carbon. Gas chromatography (Agilent 7820, USA) was used to monitor the adsorption of toluene, and the working conditions were gasification chamber temperature 150 °C, detector 160 °C, column temperature 80 °C, analysis time 5 min, high-purity nitrogen is nitrogen, H2 flow rate is 40 mL·min−1, and air flow rate is 400 mL·min−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of activated carbon modified by Mo-doped CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: 4 g of choline chloride (ChCl), 10 g of glycerol (Gly), and 0.5 g Na2MoO4 were put into a magnetic stirrer, and stirred at 90 °C for 3 h. The resulting homogeneous transparent liquid was the obtained Mo-ChCl/Gly deep eutectic solvent (DES). It was used as the precursor of CQDs after drying at 100 °C under vacuum for 4 h.

To prepare Mo-doped CQDs, 10 g of the precursor Mo-ChCl/Gly was put on an electric furnace under 500 W power, and stirred continuously at 150 °C until black blocky solid was formed. The black solid was then put into a muffle furnace and heated at 300 °C for 3 h. After the oxidative reaction, black solid powder was obtained, which was the Mo-doped CQDs powder was formed. The prepared crude Mo-doped CQDs were dissolved in deionized water, shook in ultrasonic wave for 2 h, and filtered with 0.22 μm filter membrane to obtain Mo-doped CQDs solution for later use.

In the modification of activated carbon (AC), 5 g Mo-doped CQDs solution with different content of 0.5 wt%, 1 wt%,1.5 wt%, 2 wt% and 2.5 wt% and 15 g AC were put in a Muffle furnace, respectively. They were roasted at 300 °C for 3 h, and then cooled to room temperature. The reaction products obtained was the modified AC by Mo-doped CQDs, which were named as AC/CQDs-0.5 wt%, AC/CQDs-1 wt%, AC/CQDs-1.5 wt%, AC/CQDs-2 wt%, and AC/CQDs-2.5 wt%, respectively (Scheme 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Toluene adsorption on activated carbon before and after modified
ce:section.ce:para: The toluene adsorption experiment was carried out according the previous studies [49], and the equipment was shown in Fig. 1 . It contains distribution system, fixed adsorption bed, thermal insulation device and detection system. In the adsorption process, the organic solvent toluene was evaporated, dried and mixed with N2 to prepare an organic mixed gas with a concentration of 200 ppm. The toluene was pumped into the quartz tube adsorption column equipped with 10 g activated carbon at 100 mL·min−1 under different adsorption temperature of 293 K, 30 K and 313 K. When the organic gas concentration in the outlet gas is the same as the initial concentration, and maintained for 30 min, the adsorption equilibrium was achieved, and the activated carbon was saturated. The exhaust gas was sent to the purification device.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal hazard of activated carbon before and after modification

ce:section.ce:para: The thermal stability of AC before and after CQDs modification was analyzed by STA 449 F5 thermogravimetric analyzer (TG). 5 mg AC was put in the sample crucible, and heated from 50 to 1000 °C at a heating rate of 5, 10, 15, 20, 25 °C·min−1 under N2 atmosphere, respectively. The AC after toluene adsorption sample was carried out in the same procedure to investigate the thermodynamic characterization of adsorption process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experiment on calorimetric isotherm of toluene adsorption
ce:section.ce:para: The calorimetric isotherm of the toluene adsorption by AC was studied by microcalorimeter (μRC). 0.3 g original or modified activated carbon was put into the bottle, and then toluene gas was injected at a flow rate of 20 cm·sec−1 under 25 °C for 2 h. The calorimetric isotherm of toluene adsorption at a stable temperature could be obtained. The calorimetric isotherm at different temperatures of 45 °C, 65 °C and 110 °C could be also recorded in the repeated experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experiment on the spontaneous combustion point
ce:section.ce:para: It was carried out by solid spontaneous combustion point tester according to the ASTME2021 standard and previous study [50]. 10 mg AC/CQDs with different content from 0.5% to 2.5% were put into crucibles, and the temperature of the synchronous thermal analyzer was programmed to set the desired temperature. When the AC was auto-ignited or a sudden temperature rise could be observed within 10 minutes, the temperature at which the ignition occurred was recorded as the spontaneous combustion temperature. The experimental data was plotted into a graph of the relationship between spontaneous combustion temperature and composition of CQDs.

The particle size and distribution of CQDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy. The TEM image (Fig. 2 a) shows that the prepared CQDs have good dispersion, uniform particle size and spherical morphology. The particle size distribution histogram (Fig. 2b) shows that most of the Mo-doped CQDs are in the range of 4–6 nm, with an average particle size of 4.97 nm. The valence electron transition and optical properties of CQDs were characterized by UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. S1.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to analyze the surface elemental composition and chemical state of CQDs. According to the characteristic peak distribution, Gaussian fitting was performed on the raw data to separate out the hidden peaks, as shown in Fig. 3 . The XPS full spectrum clearly shows six characteristic peaks: Na 1s (1072.8 eV), O 1s (531 eV), N 1s (398.4 eV), C 1s (285 eV), Mo3d5 (233.1 eV), Cl (198.9 eV). It shows that the CQDs contain Na, O, N, C, Mo, and Cl elements, which are consistent with the elements contained in the raw materials for the synthesis of CQDs. The high-resolution spectra of the three main elements of C, O, and Mo are further analyzed by Fig. 3a, b and c. The peaks at 284.9, 286.8, and 288.2 eV of the high-resolution spectrum of C1s can be assigned to CC/CC, CO and CO/CN, respectively; The peaks at 530.8, 531.3, and 533.7 eV of the O 1s spectrum can be assigned to CO, COH, and COC, respectively; The spectrum of Mo can be decomposed into three characteristic peaks, which are Mo6+(3d3/2), Mo6+(3d5/2), Mo4+(3d3/2) at 231.4, 232.5, and 235.6 eV, respectively, indicating that Mo may be on the surface of carbon quantum dots. It exists in the form of stable Na2MoO4, MoO3 or MoO2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of activated carbon before and after modified
ce:section.ce:para: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to observe the loading of Mo-doped CQDs on AC, as shown in Fig. 4 a. The original AC showed disordered activated carbon fiber layer structure in HR-TEM diagram. Fig. 4b shows the TEM diagram of the modified AC. It can be clearly seen that Mo-doped CQDs are successfully loaded onto the surface and the distribution is relatively uniform. Fig. 4c and d are TEM images of AC/CQDs at different magnification. It is found that crystal structures are formed on the surface of CQDs modified AC, and the lattice array in the crystal region is orderly and compact, and the lattice spacing is 0.35 nm. High crystallinity enhances the intermolecular interaction on the surface of AC and enhances the stability of activated carbon.

The changes of surface functional groups of AC before and after modification of Mo-doped CQDs were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). As shown in Fig. 5 , the strong absorption peaks at 3390, 1398 cm−1 of AC are attributed to the stretching vibration of OH bond, and the characteristic peaks at 2310, 1660, 1076 cm−1 are CH, stretching vibration of CO/COOH and CO. The absorption intensity of OH characteristic peaks (3118 cm−1) and CO/COOH characteristic peaks (1544 cm−1) in the AC/CQDs increased and redshifted compared with that of AC which may be due to the formation of hydrogen bonds between the compounds and the increase of the system energy, which leads to the red shift of the characteristic peak position. The change of the characteristic peaks of OH and CO/COOH increases the adsorption capacity and stability of AC for toluene; Compared with AC, AC/CQDs has a new characteristic absorption peak in the range of 910–450 cm−1, of which 908 cm−1 belongs to the characteristic absorption peak of MoO3, 738, 545 and 4577 cm−1 The characteristic peaks are attributed to the vibration of MoO. The FTIR results are basically consistent with XPS results.

The change process of the microcrystalline structure of AC was characterized by XRD. It can be seen from Fig. 6 that AC has diffraction peaks at 2θ = 21°, 26°, 29° and 35°. Through analysis, the diffraction peaks at 21° at 2θ are amorphous carbon structures, the diffraction peaks at 26° at 2θ are amorphous silicon oxide structures, and the diffraction peaks at 29° and 35° at 2θ are those on AC. Graphite and quartz crystal structures. After CQDs modification, new diffraction peaks appeared at 27°, 32° and 45° of 2θ, which were MoO3(0 1 1), MoO2(1 0 1) and Na2MoO4(3 5 1) crystal plane diffraction peaks, respectively. Among them, the diffraction peaks with 2θ of 32° and 45° are sharp and intense, which proves that MoO2 and Na2MoO4 exist in a highly crystalline state on the surface of AC. The XRD results further confirmed that the Mo element on the surface of AC after CQDs modification exists in the crystal form of MoO3, MoO2 and Na2MoO4, and the results of FTIR spectrum are consistent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermodynamic analysis of oxidative decomposition of activated carbon before and after modification
ce:section.ce:para: It is of great significance to improve the thermal stability of AC/CQDs on the basis of ensuring its strong adsorption capacity. Adsorption isotherm was used to study the influence of AC (such as different content of CQDs, temperature, flow rate, toluene concentration, Figs. S4–S8) on the adsorption performance of toluene before and after modification.

The adsorption isotherms and fitting line for adsorption of toluene by AC and AC/CQDs with different content of CQDs was firstly studied in Figs. S3 and S4. In the preliminary experiment of AC/CQDs preparation, the CQDs content loaded on AC ranged from 0.5 wt% to 4.5 wt%, and it was found that excessive CQDs would block the micropore structure of AC resulting in reduced adsorption capacity (the adsorption capacities of AC/CQDs-2.5 wt% reached the maximum of 39.46 cm3/g, and the adsorption capacities of AC/CQDs-4.5 wt% was 31.87 cm3/g). The adsorption isotherms of AC on toluene before and after CQDs modification have the same shape, and the adsorption amount of toluene on AC before and after modification increases with the increase of relative pressure, which is consistent with the Langmuir adsorption isotherm. This shows that AC has a pore structure dominated by micropores, and micropore filling can occur at low pressure, and the adsorption equilibrium is reached when the adsorption pressure is about 0.4. The adsorption isotherms were fitted according to the Langmuir model. According to the adsorption isotherm, the relationship between p−1 and q−1 was plotted respectively, and the fitting curve of toluene adsorption by activated carbon was obtained. qm and KL were calculated by the slope and intercept of the fitting curve. The saturated adsorption capacity of toluene by activated carbon was calculated according to adsorption kinetics Eq. (1):
(1)
q
q m
=
K L
P
1 +
K L
P

The reciprocal of both sides of Eq. (1) becomes a linear Eq. (2):
(2)
1 q
=
1
q m
+
1
K L
q m
×
1 p
where p is the equilibrium partial pressure of the adsorbent in the gas phase, q is equilibrium adsorption capacity, qm is saturated adsorption capacity and KL is the adsorption coefficient.

The analysis shows that the adsorption of AC for toluene is mainly based on uniform surface and monolayer adsorption mechanism, and the monolayer adsorption of microporous AC material is completed under very low pressure. Therefore, a good linear fitting line can be obtained by taking the adsorption amount of relative pressure of 0.005–0.1. According to formula (1) and (2), by plotting p−1 against q−1, the fitted line for the adsorption of AC and AC/CQDs to toluene at 25 °C is obtained, as shown in Fig. S4, and the adsorption results are shown in Table 1 . The prepared AC with different amount of CQDs has a good correlation coefficient with the experimental value of Langmuir isotherm (R2 > 0.98). With the increase of the amount of CQDs, the adsorption capacity of modified AC for toluene also gradually increased, and the adsorption capacity of AC/CQDs-2.5 wt% for toluene gas was the highest, increasing from 24.36 cm3∙g−1 to 39.46 cm3∙g−1, the adsorption capacity is increased by 1.6 times, indicating the modification of CQDs may increase the number of adsorption sites on the surface of AC, which is consistent with the results of specific surface area and pore volume of AC and AC/CQDs (Table S1). As is known, for adsorbents, the adsorption capacity was related with the micropore size and its ratio. In the Table S1, it was showed that the BET surface area of AC and AC/CQDs were 870.6572 and 965.4569 m2·g−1, respectively. And the micropore volume of AC/CQDs also increased from 0.2835 (AC) to 0.4292 cm3∙g−1. The increased specific surface area and micropore volume of AC/CQDs provides sufficient adsorption sites for AC to adsorb toluene, thus improving the adsorption capacity.

The results of toluene adsorption by AC and AC/CQDs with different temperature, flow rate, and toluene concentration were shown in Figs. S5–S8. It could be concluded that the adsorption capacity of absorbent not only depends surface area and pore volume of AC and AC/CQDs, but also was related to the parameters of the treatment condition. In the experiment, the toluene adsorption by AC/CQDs could achieve 39.46 cm3∙g−1 under the optimized condition of AC/CQDs-2.5 wt%, 45 °C, flow rate of 200 mL min−1, and toluene concentration of 200 ppm.

The toluene desorption of AC and AC/CQDs adsorbent was also studied to further understand the adsorption process. Compare the desorption results of AC and AC/CQDs in Fig. S8, it could be found that the desorption rate and the amount of residual toluene exhibited obvious difference. The AC/CQDs showed much lower toluene desorption rate than that of AC. It may be due to the functional groups of CQDs and more micropore volume of AC/CQDs. With introduced Mo, CO/COOH and OH functional groups on AC, it could have electronic effects with toluene molecules, which increases the difficulty of removing toluene molecules in the process of air purge removal. Meanwhile, reinforced micropore volume also brought in steric effect, which could hinder the desorption of toluene. Compared with the previous studies about toluene adsorption [51–54], the AC/CQDs absorbent showed good adsorption capacity within shorter equilibrium time at feasible temperature (Table 2 ).

Simultaneous thermal analyzer was used to characterize the effects of different CQDs additions on the thermal stability and thermal decomposition of adsorbed saturated AC. It can be seen from Fig. 7 that the weight loss trend of AC before and after modification is roughly the same, indicating that the modification of CQDs does not change the thermal decomposition process. AC begins to enter the stage of water evaporation and easily decomposed gas escape at 96 °C, resulting in weight reduction; at 322 °C, it begins to decompose under heat, and the molecular structure of AC is continuously broken at high temperature, and the quality is greatly reduced. AC/CQDs-2.5 wt% begins to thermally decompose at 392 °C, and the initial decomposition temperature of AC/CQDs-2.5 wt% is 70 °C higher than that of AC, indicating that AC/CQDs-2.5 wt% has better thermal stability. At the same time, the modification of CQDs significantly increased the carbon residue. At 800 °C, with the increase of the amount of CQDs added, the residual carbon content of AC increased from 2 wt% to 15.5 wt%, 13.5 wt% with the increase of carbon content. The results show that AC/CQDs-2.5 wt% has better thermal stability and higher residual content, which may be because CQDs form a dense and non-flammable carbon layer on the surface of AC, which effectively blocks the contact between AC and air. During the combustion process of AC/CQDs, CQDs are converted into non-flammable CO2 gas, and a protective layer of CO2 gas is formed on the surface of AC, which plays a role of self-extinguishing. Therefore, the modification of AC by CQDs effectively improves the thermal stability of activated carbon.

The change of heat during the adsorption of toluene on AC before and after modification was detected by μRC, and the effect of Mo-doped CQDs on the slow oxidation reaction of AC was explored. Fig. 8 shows the heat flow curve of unmodified AC adsorbing toluene at different temperatures. With the progress of the adsorption reaction, the heat release of AC gradually increased during the adsorption process. After reaching the adsorption equilibrium, the heat release decreased rapidly and finally stabilized. The lower the ambient temperature, the shorter the time it takes to reach equilibrium, and the smaller the thermal power reaching the extreme point. The area of the curve is the energy released. According to the calculation, the heat release in the process of toluene adsorption is 133.15 J at 25 °C, 473.25 J at 45 °C, 726.89 J at 65 °C, and 841.81 J at 110 °C. Therefore, the higher the set ambient temperature, the more heat is released during the adsorption of toluene by the unmodified AC.

Fig. 9 shows the heat flow curve of toluene adsorption by Na2MoO4 modified AC at different temperatures. The thermal power gradually increases with the increasing of temperature. After reaching the highest point, the thermal power remains balanced for a period of time when the temperature is high, and then becomes stable after a rapid decline. The lower the temperature, the shorter the time to maintain equilibrium, and the smaller the thermal power to reach the extreme point. The area of the curve is the energy released. According to the calculation, the heat released from adsorption is 125.88 J at 25 °C, 384.01 J at 45 °C, 409.53 J at 65 °C, and 951.03 J at 110 °C, respectively. Comparing the heat released during the toluene adsorption of by on AC before and after modification, the heat released by AC/CQDs is significantly reduced, which proves that the modified AC has higher stability. The Mo oxide on the surface of AC effectively could inhibit the pyrolysis of volatiles and the reaction of oxygen-containing functional groups with adsorbed gas during the toluene adsorption on AC, weaken the oxidative activity of activated carbon, and improve the stability of AC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fire safety evaluation of toluene adsorption on activated carbon before and after modification
ce:section.ce:para: In the process of VOCs adsorption, AC reacts slowly with oxygen in the air to release heat. When the ambient temperature reaches the lowest spontaneous combustion temperature of AC, spontaneous combustion will occur without the action of open flame, thus causing fire. Therefore, the lower the spontaneous combustion temperature of AC, the greater the fire risk. Fig. 10 shows that CQDs with different contents have different modification effects on the spontaneous combustion temperature of adsorbed saturated AC. With the increase of Mo-doped CQDs content, the spontaneous combustion point of AC shows an increasing trend. The spontaneous combustion temperature of the unmodified AC is 319.3 °C. When the content of Mo-doped CQDs is 2.5 wt%, the spontaneous combustion temperature of the modified AC is 378.6 °C, which is 59.3 °C higher than that of the unmodified AC. Therefore, the higher the content of Mo-doped CQDs, the higher the spontaneous combustion temperature of AC. It is confirmed that the modification of Mo-doped CQDs can effectively improve the thermal stability, which can be attributed to the increase in the content of acidic organic functional groups on the surface of modified AC, which effectively dissolves the ash on the surface of AC and reduces the content of inflammables. At the same time, the modified AC surface is loaded with Mo oxide with high crystallinity. On the one hand, the highly ordered crystal structure enhances the intermolecular force on the AC surface; On the other hand, Mo oxides can effectively inhibit the pyrolysis of AC and are also good catalysts for CQDs to form protective carbon layers.

Apparent activation energy is one of the key parameters in studying the oxidative combustion process of materials. It can indicate the difficulty of the composite reaction of materials, and then judge the spontaneous combustion tendency of AC. The higher the activation energy, the harder the AC is to be oxidized. Fig. 11 a and b show TG and DTG curves of unmodified AC at different heating rates, respectively. In the programmed heating process, the unmodified AC showed obvious weight loss, and the weight loss trend of AC under different heating rates was roughly the same. Taking the modified AC sample with a heating rate of 5 °C·min−1 as an example, the weight almost does not change before 365.79 °C with the increase of temperature. When the temperature rises to 365.79 °C, the functional groups, ash and volatile matter on the surface of AC begin to thermally decompose. When the temperature is increased to 510.7 °C, intense oxidation reaction takes place inside the AC, and the maximum mass weight loss rate is 94.3%. When the temperature was 605.4 °C, the DTG curve gradually flattened, indicating that the thermal decomposition process was over, and the AC sample reached the maximum weight loss rate, with a weight loss rate of 97.74%. It can be seen from Table 3 that the initial decomposition temperatures of unmodified AC adsorbed saturated toluene gas are 365.79 °C, 385.14 °C, 398.96 °C, 401.27 °C and 417.49 °C at the heating rates of 5, 10, 15, 20 and 25 °C·min−1, respectively. The peak temperatures were 508.84 °C, 544.91 °C, 569.22 °C, 591.76 °C and 600.49 °C, respectively. The initial decomposition temperature and peak temperature of the modified AC increased with the increase of the heating rate, indicating that the higher the heating rate, the longer the initial decomposition time of AC. This is because the thermal conductivity of AC powder is small. In the process of rapid heating of the instrument, the weak heat transfer ability leads to the slow heating rate of AC inside, so that the oxidation reaction in the system goes to the high temperature area, and the release of heat is also delayed.

Flynn-wall-ozawa (FWO) integral method was used to characterize the thermal kinetics of AC before and after modification. According to formula (3), with the heating rate of 5 °C·min−1, 10 °C·min−1, 15 °C·min−1, 20 °C·min−1, 25 °C·min−1 The reciprocal of the peak temperature is the abscissa, and the log(β) is the ordinate. After linear fitting, the apparent activation energy E of the unmodified AC was calculated using Eq. (1).

FWO equation:
(3)
lg
( β )
= lg
AE
R · g ( α )
- 2.315 - 0.4567
E R
·
1 T
where β is the heating rate (°C∙min−1); A is the pre-exponential factor(1∙min−1); E is the apparent activation energy (kJ∙mol−1); R is the ideal gas constant (J·mol−1·k−1); g(a) is the integral form of the reaction mechanism function; T is the thermodynamic temperature, °C.

As shown in the Fig. 12 , this analysis method does not need to make any assumptions about the reaction mechanism, and the fitted straight line can be obtained as y = −2266.164x + 5.154. According to the formula, we can obtain −0.4567(E/R) = −2266.164, and R2 is 0.9986. The calculation shows that E = 41.254 kJ∙mol−1.

The TG and DTG curves of the modified AC adsorbing saturated toluene gas at different heating rates were also detected, the activation energy of AC before and after modification were compared, and the effect of Mo-doped CQDs on the oxidation difficulty of AC was explored. The AC was modified by CQDs with 2.5 wt%, and the thermodynamic characteristics of the modified AC after adsorbing saturated toluene gas were characterized by TG and DTG curves. Fig. 13 a and b were the TG and DTG curves of AC/CQDs at different heating rates, respectively. Under programmed heating, the change trend of the five TG curves is basically the same under different heating rates. The higher the heating rate, the higher the peak temperature and the higher the initial decomposition temperature. The characteristic parameters of TG and DTG curves of AC/CQDs were shown in Table 4 . The initial decomposition temperatures of modified AC adsorbed saturated toluene gas were 392.64 °C, 403.98 °C, 419.53 °C, 401.27 °C and 424.16 °C at the heating rates of 5, 10, 15, 20 and 25 °C·min−1, respectively. The peak temperature was 547.64 °C, 566.37 °C, 594.20 °C, 604.25 °C, 628.16 °C. The thermal stability of modified AC is analyzed by taking the curve with a heating rate of 5 °C·min−1 as an example. Combined with the DTG curve of AC/CQDs, the TG curve of AC/CQDs changed slowly when the temperature was lower than 392.64 °C. When the temperature is between 392.64 °C and 585.6 °C, the weight loss of AC/CQDs is at a relatively fast stage; When the temperature is 585.6 °C, the weight loss rate is 86.1%. When the temperature exceeds 615.1 °C, the TG curve does not change significantly, the weight loss rate is close to 0, and the final weight loss rate is 90.1%. The modified AC adsorbed toluene can exist stably at a temperature below 392.64 °C.

As shown in Table 4 and Fig. 14 , according to the peak temperature of AC/CQDs at different heating rates, log (β) was used to curve fit Tp−1, and y = −2908.34x + 6.0706, the slope was −(E/R) = −2908.34, R2 = 0.97966, E = 52.94 kJ∙mol−1. Therefore, the activation energy of AC/CQDs was 52.94 kJ∙mol−1, which was increased by 11.69 kJ·mol−1 after modification compared with that of before modification, and the activation energy was significantly increased, indicating that the oxidation reaction of AC required higher energy, was not easy to decompose when heated, and had high thermal stability. In the combustion stage of fixed carbon, the activation energy required by AC before modification was 41.25 kJ∙mol−1, and that required by AC after modification was 52.94 kJ·mol−1. The activation energy of AC after modification was 1.28 times higher than that of before modification. The increase of reaction activation energy indicated that the minimum energy required for oxidation reaction in this system increased, and AC was not easy to spontaneously ignite, thus the safety is enhanced. The improvement of the activation energy of AC after modification is due to the fact that AC/CQDs increases the crystalline area on the surface of AC, the structure of the crystalline area is dense, the molecular arrangement is tight, and the intermolecular interaction has more strong connection points. The AC structure is more stable, and it takes more energy to break the highly ordered crystal structure. On the other hand, Mo oxide can effectively inhibit the thermal decomposition process of organic functional groups on the surface of AC. Therefore, the increase of the activation energy of the modified AC greatly reduces the spontaneous combustion tendency of the AC, and the thermal stability is stronger.

The four thermodynamic parameters of AC before and after modification are used as the characteristic parameters of the relative fire safety evaluation model, and the relationship between each parameter and the fire risk is used for safety evaluation. Taking the unmodified AC as the reference, a relative fire safety performance evaluation model is established, comprehensively evaluate the fire spontaneous combustion risk of AC with each parameter. Relative Fire Safety Factor is referred to as RFSF, and the formula is as follows (4):
(4)
RFSF =
1 4
(
E i
E 0
+
1 /
DTG
p - i
1 /
DTG
p - 0
+
T
p - i
T
P - 0
+
T
z - i
T
z - 0
)
where RFSF is the Relative Fire Safety Factor: E0, DTGp-0, Tp-0 and Tz-0 are the activation energy, maximum mass loss rate, peak temperature and spontaneous combustion temperature of the unmodified AC, respectively E i, DTG p-I, T p-i and T z-i are the activation energy, maximum mass loss rate, peak temperature and spontaneous combustion temperature of AC/CQDs, respectively.

In this paper, the characteristic parameters of AC measured at a heating rate of 5 °C min−1 are uniformly selected to calculate the relative fire safety factor. The relative fire safety factors of AC before and after modification were shown in Table 5 .

The RFSF values of AC before and after modification of Mo-doped CQDs were 1 and 1.15, respectively, indicating that the modification of Mo-doped CQDs effectively improved the thermal stability of activated carbon. The relative fire safety evaluation model comprehensively considers various characteristic parameters. In the evaluation of AC fire safety performance, the calculation process is simple and can clearly show the factors affecting the fire safety performance of materials. This model can be used to evaluate the fire safety of other ACs or modified AC, showing efficient safety performance and providing essential data for engineering applications.

In this work, Na2MoO4, glycerol and choline chloride were used as raw materials to synthesize Mo-doped CQDs by a one-step hot solvent method. The modified AC was impregnated and toluene adsorption and thermal stability experiments were carried out. The thermal stability and thermal decomposition process of AC during adsorption of toluene before and after modification were studied by TG and μRC; Based on spontaneous combustion temperature test, TG and DTG experimental parameters, the relative fire safety evaluation model was established for the comprehensively evaluation of the fire risk of activated carbon. CQDs show a good application in the modification of AC. Mo-doped CQDs modified AC can effectively improve the adsorption performance and thermal stability of AC, greatly reduce the spontaneous combustion tendency, and broaden the practical application of AC in the field of gas adsorption.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Science Foundation of China (52004080), Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (E2021208033, E2022208046), Hebei University of Science and Technology graduate Innovation Ability training funding project (XJCXZZSS2022013), Hebei Province graduate student Innovation ability training funding projectHebei Province graduate student Innovation ability training funding project (CXZZSS2023091).

ce:section.ce:para: The data that supports the findings of this study are available in the supplementary material of this article.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jiec.2023.07.034.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary material to this article:

B. Lei, B. Liu, H. Zhang , L. Yan, and H. Xie. Environ. Sci. 882020122132.
J. Wang, Z. Wu, Q. Niu, L. Liu, L. Yang, M. Fu, D. Ye and P., ChenJ. Hazard. Mater.4152021125753.
